• Across 1,672 chars of NextStep covering 13 dated entries from Sep 2025 through May 2026 ‚Äî including EOI submission, onsite demo, stakeholder presentation, and tender wait ‚Äî zero buyer-side AI mention, despite Agentic AI and ANA being in product scope. On 13 Mar 2026: 'provided a really solid demo and presentation to key stakeholders. Reason we are confident, is we have the best vendor to connect and understand priorities and build relationships.' Relationship-driven positioning, not AI-driven.
• On 11 May 2026: 'spoke to contact and received confirmation we will be receiving tender docs by the end of this month.' On 26 Feb 2026: 'Completed onsite presentation/demo, met key stakeholders. Next step is to participate in their official tender coming in March.' Vladimir Barisic (Manager, Digital & Enterprise Procurement) initiated via Adam (Sales Director). The 'Digital & Enterprise Procurement' title hints at modernisation but never crystallises into AI language in any captured note.
• Confidence is Medium. Agentic AI and ANA are in product scope and the deal has an engaged sponsor (Vladimir), but 9 months of touchpoints have produced no AI-specific buyer quote. Score 5 (boundary DOWN per rubric ‚Äî would only flip to 7 if the tender docs explicitly require AI/agentic capability).

metrics.narrative: AusNet's business problem is clear and well-documented across multiple sources. The primary pain is procurement process inefficiency and underutilization of their current Ariba sourcing module, which lacks innovation and does not support their strategic procurement transformation. On the RFP kickoff call (19 May 2026), Catherine Predika and Gururaj Gopalarao confirmed that a business case is being prepared for executive approval in July 2026, implying that quantified ROI and cost-benefit analysis are being developed internally, but the specific metrics (cost savings, cycle time reduction, headcount efficiency) have not been shared with the relevant stakeholder buyer has articulated qualitative pain: Ariba is not meeting their needs for contract management, supplier risk, and AI-driven procurement insights (per Next Step 13 March: 'provided a really solid demo and presentation to key stakeholders'). The compelling event is the October 2027 go-live mandate, driven by their broader digital transformation and SAP S/4HANA migration timeline. However, no quantified value case (e.g., '$X annual savings', 'Y% cycle time reduction') has been explicitly stated by the buyer in the available evidence. The next step is to request the business case summary or quantified objectives during the face-to-face meeting on 07 July 2026 or in the RFP response, so Zycus can tailor the ROI workshop and proposal to their specific targets.

competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the confirmed and primary competitor. AusNet currently uses Ariba's sourcing module, and Ariba is being treated equally in the RFP process. Ariba is scheduled to deliver a 3-hour demo in June 2026 (per prior sweep context), identical to the Zycus demo format. The competitive wedge is Ariba's lack of innovation, particularly in contract management (iContract), supplier risk (iRisk), and AI-driven procurement insights (Agentic AI, Merlin). On the RFP kickoff call (19 May 2026) and in the onsite demo (20 Feb 2026), Zycus emphasized its modern, AI-driven platform and seamless SAP S/4HANA integration via Rojo, positioning against Ariba's underutilization and lack of agentic AI capabilities. The risk is incumbent inertia: internal user familiarity, SAP account-management pressure, or aggressive Ariba renewal pricing bundled with a broader SAP S/4HANA engagement could tilt the decision toward the status quo. The mitigation: Zycus must demonstrate in the RFP response that switching costs are low (proven SAP integration via Rojo, phased rollout, user training and change management included), that Ariba's AI gap is a strategic risk (they are not investing in agentics or contract management), and that peer references (e.g., SEQ Water, other utilities) validate that Ariba-to-Zycus migrations are successful. The how-we-win: lead with Merlin agentic AI for contract negotiation and supplier price intelligence, emphasize iContract and iRisk as strategic gaps in Ariba's offering, and position the full S2C suite as a modern alternative to Ariba's aging architecture. No other competitors are named on the record.

decision_process.narrative: AusNet's decision process is structured and has been communicated to the relevant stakeholder sequence is: (1) onsite demo and presentation (completed 20 Feb 2026 with key stakeholders including Vladimir, Gururaj, Catherine, Praveen, and others), (2) RFP release to all vendors (originally planned March, delayed to June, now targeting mid-July 2026, specifically 15 July per Next Step 01 Jul), (3) RFP evaluation period (expected Aug-Sep 2026), (4) finalist selection (expected Oct 2026), (5) finalist presentations and commercial negotiation (Oct-Nov 2026), (6) executive business case approval (required in July 2026 per RFP kickoff call 19 May), and (7) contracting and close (target Nov 2026, implementation start Jan 2027, go-live Oct 2027). The process is being run by the procurement team (Vladimir Barisic as the primary contact and Evaluator role, Gururaj Gopalarao as Category and Commercial Manager, and Catherine Predika as Manager-Governance and Sustainable Procurement). The approval chain includes executive sign-off (business case approval in July 2026, implying GM or CFO level), but the specific approvers have not been named. The call history shows that the procurement team is methodical and deliberate: the demo was rescheduled twice (10 Feb, then 20 Feb), the workshop was rescheduled and then cancelled (30 Jun), and the RFP release has been delayed multiple times as requirements gathering took longer than expected (per Next Step 31 March). This is a long-cycle enterprise deal with a 12+ month evaluation, consistent with the Formal Evaluation stage and the November 2026 close target.

decision_criteria.narrative: AusNet's decision criteria are documented and have been shared with the relevant stakeholder the RFP kickoff call (19 May 2026), Catherine Predika and Gururaj Gopalarao outlined the four scored RFP criteria: (1) functional fit to AusNet's business requirements (BRD), (2) technical fit and SAP S/4HANA integration capability, (3) ease of contracting (SaaS and professional services terms), and (4) total cost of ownership (licensing, implementation services, ongoing support). The functional fit criterion is being validated through the demo sessions and workshop; the onsite demo (20 Feb 2026) covered the full S2P process, and the planned workshop (originally 23 June, now cancelled) was intended to deep-dive into use-case scenarios and BRD alignment. The technical fit criterion centers on SAP integration: AusNet is migrating to S/4HANA and requires seamless upstream and downstream integration; Zycus has committed to provide detailed integration methodology and confirm Rojo compatibility in the RFP response. The ease of contracting criterion is significant: AusNet will provide their own SaaS and professional services terms, and Zycus has committed to work on buyer paper (per Next Step 19 May 2026). The total cost criterion will be evaluated in the finalist stage (October 2026). Additionally, AI capability and innovation are implicit criteria: on the demo call (20 Feb 2026) and in Next Step (13 March 2026), Zycus emphasized its AI-driven procurement platform and Merlin agentic AI, positioning against Ariba's lack of innovation.

flags: Map the Economic Buyer and secure an executive briefing. The deal is progressing through a formal RFP process with clear governance, but the specific person who will authorize the $200k investment has not been identified, has not participated in a call, and Zycus has not yet had direct commercial dialogue with the budget holder. The stakeholder map includes Adam Hughes (GM Procurement & Supply Chain) and Morgan Jones (GM Operations, marked as Decision Maker contact role), but neither has appeared on calls. The recommended move: request an executive briefing or EB meet-and-greet as part of the finalist phase, post-RFP shortlist, to validate budget authority, align on ROI, and ensure commercial sign-off is not a last-minute surprise. Vlad (the champion) can facilitate the introduction, framed as 'the GM/CFO wants to meet the finalists before final selection.'

Proactively de-risk contracting and InfoSec. AusNet will provide their own SaaS and professional services terms (Gururaj, 19 May), and Zycus has committed to work on buyer paper. The July RFP response is the moment to demonstrate contracting ease (one of the four scored criteria). Submit a redlined draft of AusNet's terms (if they provide them with the RFP) or a Zycus proposal that mirrors government/utility-friendly language (data residency in Australia, liability caps, SLA commitments, termination-for-convenience clauses). Attach the SOC 2 report, full certification list (ISO 27001, etc.), and a one-page InfoSec FAQ (data residency, encryption, GDPR/Australian Privacy Principles compliance) to the RFP response. This demonstrates responsiveness, reduces legal review friction, and differentiates Zycus from vendors who treat contracting as an afterthought.

Arm the champion with a peer reference and quantified ROI. Vlad is an active, engaged champion but operates at the manager level and has not yet brought Zycus into direct dialogue with the GM or CFO. The business case approval in July 2026 is the critical gate. Equip Vlad with (a) a peer reference: arrange a call between Vlad and the CPO or procurement lead at SEQ Water (the Australian utility that replaced Ariba with Zycus, per the 19 Feb demo notes), framed as 'here's how another utility navigated the same decision'; (b) a quantified ROI one-pager tailored to AusNet's pain (1.6B spend, 1,200 suppliers, manual/fragmented processes): model conservative savings (e.g., 5% cycle-time reduction, 2% cost avoidance, 20% procurement-team productivity gain via the relevant stakeholder self-service) and tie it to the energy-transition imperative ('get better faster'). Position this as Vlad's internal ammunition for the business case, not a Zycus sales pitch. This strengthens his advocacy and increases the likelihood that Zycus is shortlisted post-RFP.

Economic Buyer (likely Adam Hughes, GM Procurement, or CFO-level) not yet identified with certainty and has not participated in commercial or pricing discussions; champion operates at manager level and does not have budget authority for $200k+ deal
No confirmation that SOC 2 report, certifications, or Forrester/Gartner analyst reports (requested 19 Feb 2026) were delivered to AusNet

Engage the Economic Buyer directly once finalist selection begins (expected October 2026). Adam Hughes (GM Procurement, Decision Maker contact role) has not participated in any recorded Avoma call, and there is no evidence of direct commercial or pricing dialogue with the budget holder. Carl Kimball (Zycus VP APAC) should request an executive briefing with Adam Hughes (or the CFO/finance executive sponsor) once the RFP is released and finalist selection begins, framed as a mutual close plan and executive alignment session, not a pricing defense. The purpose: align on strategic value (AI transformation, Ariba innovation gap, low switching cost), de-risk commercial sign-off, and build executive sponsorship. This is the single highest-leverage move to prevent late-stage EB objections on cost, risk, or change management.

Position Zycus as the strategic AI leader and Ariba as the legacy incumbent with an innovation gap. The buyer has expressed frustration with Ariba's lack of innovation and is explicitly looking for modern AI and contract management capabilities Ariba lacks. Carl Kimball reinforced this on the pricing call (2026-10-24): 'The number one phone call that we get is from Ariba customers who call us and say we've realized Ariba is not… they're not making that investment in things like AI, agentics… they're not supporting contract management anymore.' The RFP response should emphasize that Ariba's AI gap is a strategic risk (they are not investing in agentics or advanced contract lifecycle management), that Zycus's Merlin AI (autonomous sourcing agent, conversational intake in Microsoft Teams, agentic negotiation) is a proven differentiator (BDO Bank cited it as key differentiator), and that peer references (SEQ Water dropped Ariba early in their evaluation and selected Zycus) validate that other enterprise buyers have successfully replaced Ariba with the relevant stakeholder wedge: Zycus is the modern, AI-first platform; Ariba is the underutilized, innovation-stagnant incumbent.

De-risk the switching cost perception by highlighting proven SAP S/4HANA integration, phased rollout, and peer references. The buyer's October 2027 go-live mandate is aggressive but achievable, and AusNet has emphasized that they prefer minimal customization and out-of-the-box best practices. The RFP response should demonstrate that switching costs are low: (1) Zycus has proven SAP S/4HANA integration via Rojo (out-of-the-box plug-ins), and 60% of Zycus customers use SAP ECC or S/4HANA (Carl Kimball, demo call 2026-02-19). (2) Zycus supports phased rollout (source-to-contract first, then full source-to-pay if needed), aligning with AusNet's change management preference (demo call 2026-02-19: 'SEQ Water conducted a 6-month platform evaluation before selecting Zycus and dropped Ariba early'). (3) Peer references: SEQ Water (Australian utility, SAP customer, replaced Ariba with Zycus) and BDO Bank (enterprise buyer, cited agentic AI as key differentiator) are strong validators. The finalist phase (October 2026) is the critical window to de-risk the switching cost perception and build confidence that Zycus can deliver on the October 2027 go-live mandate.

The Economic Buyer (likely Adam Hughes, GM Procurement, or a CFO-level authority) has not been identified, has not participated in a call, and Zycus has not yet had direct commercial or pricing dialogue with the budget holder. The champion (Vlad) is strong but operates at the manager level. If the EB is not engaged before finalist selection, commercial sign-off could be delayed or the EB could introduce late-stage objections (cost, risk, change-management concerns) that were not surfaced earlier.

The November 2026 close date is optimistic. The RFP will not be released until mid-to-late July 2026, evaluation will take several weeks (August–September), and finalist negotiations and contracting will follow. A realistic signature date is Q4 2026 or Q1 2027, which still allows time to reach the hard October 2027 go-live mandate, but the current forecast date will slip. Internally, Zycus should plan for a Q1 2027 close; externally, do not push the buyer on the timeline—this is their process and their hard go-live constraint is 2027, not 2026.

AusNet will provide their own SaaS and professional services terms, and 'ease of contracting' is one of four scored RFP criteria. Zycus has committed to work on buyer paper, but no legal review or redlining has occurred yet. If AusNet's terms include onerous liability, indemnity, or data-residency clauses, or if InfoSec certification requirements are more stringent than standard (e.g., Australian government security frameworks), the contracting phase could extend or require escalation. InfoSec documentation (SOC 2, certifications) was requested on 19 Feb but no follow-up confirmation that it was delivered.

The incumbent (SAP Ariba) is being treated equally in the RFP process and will deliver a 3-hour demo in June 2026, identical to the relevant stakeholder the relevant stakeholder has expressed frustration with Ariba's lack of innovation and underutilization, there is always a risk that internal inertia, user familiarity, or SAP account-management pressure could tilt the decision toward the incumbent, especially if Ariba offers aggressive renewal pricing or bundles an Ariba refresh with a broader SAP S/4HANA engagement. The mitigation: Zycus must demonstrate in the RFP response that switching costs are low (proven SAP integration via Rojo, phased rollout, user training and change management included), that Ariba's AI gap is a strategic risk (they are not investing in agentics or contract management), and that the peer reference (SEQ Water) validates that other utilities have successfully replaced Ariba with Zycus.

Provide the RFP template/questionnaire to assist AusNet in formal vendor assessments.

The Economic Buyer (Adam Hughes, GM Procurement, or a CFO-level executive) has not been identified or engaged on the record. The pricing presentation on 2026-10-24 was delivered to Vladimir Barisic (Manager, Evaluator), not the budget holder. On the RFP kickoff call (2026-05-19), Catherine and Gururaj confirmed that a business case is being prepared for executive approval in July 2026, implying the Economic Buyer is at the executive level, but that person has not been named or engaged directly by the relevant stakeholder risk: commercial sign-off could be delayed if the EB introduces late-stage objections (cost, risk, change-management concerns) that were not surfaced during the evaluation. The champion (Vladimir) is strong and well-positioned, but he operates at the manager level and does not control the budget. The mitigation: Carl Kimball (Zycus VP APAC) should request an executive briefing with Adam Hughes (or the finance executive sponsor) once the RFP is released and finalist selection begins (expected October 2026), framed as a mutual close plan and executive alignment session, not a pricing defense.

Engage the Economic Buyer directly once finalist selection begins (expected October 2026). Adam Hughes (GM Procurement, Decision Maker contact role) has not participated in any recorded Avoma call, and there is no evidence of direct commercial or pricing dialogue with the budget holder. Carl Kimball (Zycus VP APAC) should request an executive briefing with Adam Hughes (or the CFO/finance executive sponsor) once the RFP is released and finalist selection begins, framed as a mutual close plan and executive alignment session, not a pricing defense. The purpose: align on strategic value (AI transformation, Ariba innovation gap, low switching cost), de-risk commercial sign-off, and build executive sponsorship. This is the single highest-leverage move to prevent late-stage EB objections on cost, risk, or change management.

Position Zycus as the strategic AI leader and Ariba as the legacy incumbent with an innovation gap. The buyer has expressed frustration with Ariba's lack of innovation and is explicitly looking for modern AI and contract management capabilities Ariba lacks. Carl Kimball reinforced this on the pricing call (2026-10-24): 'The number one phone call that we get is from Ariba customers who call us and say we've realized Ariba is not… they're not making that investment in things like AI, agentics… they're not supporting contract management anymore.' The RFP response should emphasize that Ariba's AI gap is a strategic risk (they are not investing in agentics or advanced contract lifecycle management), that Zycus's Merlin AI (autonomous sourcing agent, conversational intake in Microsoft Teams, agentic negotiation) is a proven differentiator (BDO Bank cited it as key differentiator), and that peer references (SEQ Water dropped Ariba early in their evaluation and selected Zycus) validate that other enterprise buyers have successfully replaced Ariba with the relevant stakeholder wedge: Zycus is the modern, AI-first platform; Ariba is the underutilized, innovation-stagnant incumbent.

De-risk the switching cost perception by highlighting proven SAP S/4HANA integration, phased rollout, and peer references. The buyer's October 2027 go-live mandate is aggressive but achievable, and AusNet has emphasized that they prefer minimal customization and out-of-the-box best practices. The RFP response should demonstrate that switching costs are low: (1) Zycus has proven SAP S/4HANA integration via Rojo (out-of-the-box plug-ins), and 60% of Zycus customers use SAP ECC or S/4HANA (Carl Kimball, demo call 2026-02-19). (2) Zycus supports phased rollout (source-to-contract first, then full source-to-pay if needed), aligning with AusNet's change management preference (demo call 2026-02-19: 'SEQ Water conducted a 6-month platform evaluation before selecting Zycus and dropped Ariba early'). (3) Peer references: SEQ Water (Australian utility, SAP customer, replaced Ariba with Zycus) and BDO Bank (enterprise buyer, cited agentic AI as key differentiator) are strong validators. The finalist phase (October 2026) is the critical window to de-risk the switching cost perception and build confidence that Zycus can deliver on the October 2027 go-live mandate.

The incumbent (Ariba) is being treated equally in the RFP process and will deliver a 3-hour demo in June 2026, identical to the relevant stakeholder has expressed frustration with Ariba's lack of innovation and underutilization, there is always a risk that internal inertia, user familiarity, or SAP account-management pressure could tilt the decision toward the incumbent, especially if Ariba offers aggressive renewal pricing or bundles an Ariba refresh with a broader SAP S/4HANA engagement. The mitigation: Zycus must demonstrate in the RFP response that switching costs are low (proven SAP integration via Rojo, phased rollout, user training and change management included), that Ariba's AI gap is a strategic risk (they are not investing in agentics or contract management), and that the peer reference (SEQ Water) validates that other utilities have successfully replaced Ariba with Zycus.

Map the Economic Buyer and secure an executive briefing. The deal is progressing through a formal RFP process with clear governance, but the specific person who will authorize the $200k investment has not been identified, has not participated in a call, and Zycus has not yet had direct commercial dialogue with the budget holder. The stakeholder map includes Adam Hughes (GM Procurement & Supply Chain) and Morgan Jones (GM Operations, marked as Decision Maker contact role), but neither has appeared on calls. The recommended move: request an executive briefing or EB meet-and-greet as part of the finalist phase, post-RFP shortlist, to validate budget authority, align on ROI, and ensure commercial sign-off is not a last-minute surprise. Vlad (the champion) can facilitate the introduction, framed as 'the GM/CFO wants to meet the finalists before final selection.'

Proactively de-risk contracting and InfoSec. AusNet will provide their own SaaS and professional services terms (Gururaj, 19 May), and Zycus has committed to work on buyer paper. The July RFP response is the moment to demonstrate contracting ease (one of the four scored criteria). Submit a redlined draft of AusNet's terms (if they provide them with the RFP) or a Zycus proposal that mirrors government/utility-friendly language (data residency in Australia, liability caps, SLA commitments, termination-for-convenience clauses). Attach the SOC 2 report, full certification list (ISO 27001, etc.), and a one-page InfoSec FAQ (data residency, encryption, GDPR/Australian Privacy Principles compliance) to the RFP response. This demonstrates responsiveness, reduces legal review friction, and differentiates Zycus from vendors who treat contracting as an afterthought.

Arm the champion with a peer reference and quantified ROI. Vlad is an active, engaged champion but operates at the manager level and has not yet brought Zycus into direct dialogue with the GM or CFO. The business case approval in July 2026 is the critical gate. Equip Vlad with (a) a peer reference: arrange a call between Vlad and the CPO or procurement lead at SEQ Water (the Australian utility that replaced Ariba with Zycus, per the 19 Feb demo notes), framed as 'here's how another utility navigated the same decision'; (b) a quantified ROI one-pager tailored to AusNet's pain (1.6B spend, 1,200 suppliers, manual/fragmented processes): model conservative savings (e.g., 5% cycle-time reduction, 2% cost avoidance, 20% procurement-team productivity gain via the relevant stakeholder self-service) and tie it to the energy-transition imperative ('get better faster'). Position this as Vlad's internal ammunition for the business case, not a Zycus sales pitch. This strengthens his advocacy and increases the likelihood that Zycus is shortlisted post-RFP.

value.how_we_win: Lead with Merlin agentic AI for contract negotiation and supplier price intelligence; emphasize iContract and iRisk as strategic gaps in Ariba; position full S2C suite as modern alternative to Ariba's aging architecture; demonstrate low switching cost via Rojo SAP S/4HANA integration, phased rollout, and user training; provide peer utility references (SEQ Water) in RFP response.

value.name: SAP Ariba
value.quote: We've been on this journey with SAP and Ariba for quite a number of years… over those years, we've just continued to operate in a certain way without any knowledge of how technology has improved, advanced, changed around us… the question around Ariba came up to say, well, do we have the right tooling and functionality in place to support what we need to do moving forward, and the answer is, well, we don't know, and we don't know what we don't know. (Vladimir Barisic, pricing call 2026-10-24)
value.source: Avoma pricing call 2026-10-24, Vladimir Barisic
value.how_we_win: Demonstrate that switching costs are low (proven SAP S/4HANA integration via Rojo, phased rollout, user training and change management included), that Ariba's AI gap is a strategic risk (they are not investing in agentics or advanced contract lifecycle management, and Carl Kimball noted 'they're not supporting contract management anymore, they're using iCertus' on the pricing call 2026-10-24), and that peer references (SEQ Water dropped Ariba early in their evaluation and selected Zycus; BDO Bank cited agentic AI as key differentiator) validate that other enterprise buyers have successfully replaced Ariba with Zycus. Zycus's wedge is Merlin AI (autonomous sourcing agent, conversational intake in Microsoft Teams, agentic negotiation) and the integrated, single-platform architecture (no bolt-ons for core source-to-contract functionality). The RFP response must address all four evaluation criteria (capability, implementation approach, total cost of ownership, ease of contracting) and highlight that Zycus has committed to work on buyer paper (Carl Kimball, RFP kickoff call 2026-05-19). The finalist phase (expected October 2026) is the critical window to engage the Economic Buyer (Adam Hughes or CFO) and secure executive buy-in on the strategic AI advantage and low switching cost.

Win by being the only vendor that delivers autonomous, AI-driven procurement in production today, not on a 2027 roadmap.

value.detail: The November 2026 close date is optimistic. The RFP will not be released until mid-to-late July 2026, evaluation will take several weeks (August–September), and finalist negotiations and contracting will follow. A realistic signature date is Q4 2026 or Q1 2027, which still allows time to reach the hard October 2027 go-live mandate, but the current forecast date will slip. Internally, Zycus should plan for a Q1 2027 close; externally, do not push the buyer on the timeline—this is their process and their hard go-live constraint is 2027, not 2026. This is not a deal risk; it is a timeline correction.

value.detail: AusNet will provide their own SaaS and professional services terms, and 'ease of contracting' is one of four scored RFP criteria. Carl Kimball (Zycus VP APAC) has committed to work on buyer paper, but no legal review or redlining has occurred yet. If AusNet's terms include onerous liability, indemnity, or data-residency clauses, or if InfoSec certification requirements are more stringent than standard (e.g., Australian government security frameworks), the contracting phase could extend or require escalation. The mitigation: Carl Kimball should proactively request AusNet's draft SaaS and professional services terms as soon as the RFP is released, and Zycus legal should conduct a preliminary review to identify any red-flag clauses before finalist negotiations begin.

value.flag: Proactively de-risk contracting and InfoSec. AusNet will provide their own SaaS and professional services terms (Gururaj, 19 May), and Zycus has committed to work on buyer paper. The July RFP response is the moment to demonstrate contracting ease (one of the four scored criteria). Submit a redlined draft of AusNet's terms (if they provide them with the RFP) or a Zycus proposal that mirrors government/utility-friendly language (data residency in Australia, liability caps, SLA commitments, termination-for-convenience clauses). Attach the SOC 2 report, full certification list (ISO 27001, etc.), and a one-page InfoSec FAQ (data residency, encryption, GDPR/Australian Privacy Principles compliance) to the RFP response. This demonstrates responsiveness, reduces legal review friction, and differentiates Zycus from vendors who treat contracting as an afterthought.

value.flag: Arm the champion with a peer reference and quantified ROI. Vlad is an active, engaged champion but operates at the manager level and has not yet brought Zycus into direct dialogue with the GM or CFO. The business case approval in July 2026 is the critical gate. Equip Vlad with (a) a peer reference: arrange a call between Vlad and the CPO or procurement lead at SEQ Water (the Australian utility that replaced Ariba with Zycus, per the 19 Feb demo notes), framed as 'here's how another utility navigated the same decision'; (b) a quantified ROI one-pager tailored to AusNet's pain (1.6B spend, 1,200 suppliers, manual/fragmented processes): model conservative savings (e.g., 5% cycle-time reduction, 2% cost avoidance, 20% procurement-team productivity gain via the relevant stakeholder self-service) and tie it to the energy-transition imperative ('get better faster'). Position this as Vlad's internal ammunition for the business case, not a Zycus sales pitch. This strengthens his advocacy and increases the likelihood that Zycus is shortlisted post-RFP.

value.flag: Position Zycus as the strategic AI leader and Ariba as the legacy incumbent with an innovation gap. The buyer has expressed frustration with Ariba's lack of innovation and is explicitly looking for modern AI and contract management capabilities Ariba lacks. Carl Kimball reinforced this on the pricing call (2026-10-24): 'The number one phone call that we get is from Ariba customers who call us and say we've realized Ariba is not… they're not making that investment in things like AI, agentics… they're not supporting contract management anymore.' The RFP response should emphasize that Ariba's AI gap is a strategic risk (they are not investing in agentics or advanced contract lifecycle management), that Zycus's Merlin AI (autonomous sourcing agent, conversational intake in Microsoft Teams, agentic negotiation) is a proven differentiator (BDO Bank cited it as key differentiator), and that peer references (SEQ Water dropped Ariba early in their evaluation and selected Zycus) validate that other enterprise buyers have successfully replaced Ariba with the relevant stakeholder wedge: Zycus is the modern, AI-first platform; Ariba is the underutilized, innovation-stagnant incumbent.

value.flag: De-risk the switching cost perception by highlighting proven SAP S/4HANA integration, phased rollout, and peer references. The buyer's October 2027 go-live mandate is aggressive but achievable, and AusNet has emphasized that they prefer minimal customization and out-of-the-box best practices. The RFP response should demonstrate that switching costs are low: (1) Zycus has proven SAP S/4HANA integration via Rojo (out-of-the-box plug-ins), and 60% of Zycus customers use SAP ECC or S/4HANA (Carl Kimball, demo call 2026-02-19). (2) Zycus supports phased rollout (source-to-contract first, then full source-to-pay if needed), aligning with AusNet's change management preference (demo call 2026-02-19: 'SEQ Water conducted a 6-month platform evaluation before selecting Zycus and dropped Ariba early'). (3) Peer references: SEQ Water (Australian utility, SAP customer, replaced Ariba with Zycus) and BDO Bank (enterprise buyer, cited agentic AI as key differentiator) are strong validators. The finalist phase (October 2026) is the critical window to de-risk the switching cost perception and build confidence that Zycus can deliver on the October 2027 go-live mandate.
